Summary: FOXP3-expressing regulatory T cells can suppress autoimmune responses and, within the tumor microenvironment, can exert suppressive functions, forming physical, metabolic, and trafficking barriers to exclude pro-inflammatory cells from the TME. In the tumor microenvironment, inducing regulatory T cell fragility is associated with lower FOXP3 expression and with enhanced production of tumor-killing cytokines such as interferon-gamma. In Ifitm3 fl/fl Foxp3 YFP-cre mice, deletion of Ifitm3 specifically in Treg cells did not change the percentage of FOXP3+ Treg cells among CD4+ T cells or FOXP3 protein levels compared with wild-type mice. In ESCA tumor tissue, tumor-infiltrating regulatory T cells divided by FOXP3 expression into FOXP3 low and FOXP3 high groups, and FOXP3 high TI-Treg subsets showed higher IFITM3 expression. Correlation analysis of IFITM3 and FOXP3 expression in TI-Tregs from ESCA patients indicated that IFITM3 may participate in regulation of Treg cell function and stability in the tumor microenvironment.

Summary: FOXP3 is the lineage-determining transcription factor for regulatory T cells. FOXP3 forms a large molecular complex with multiple transcription factors and enzymatic subunits to dynamically regulate the development and function of regulatory T cells. FOXP3+ Treg cells maintain immune homeostasis in healthy people. In tumours, FOXP3+ Treg cells have strong immunosuppressive function that may promote immune escape of cancer cells and tumour development. Tumour-derived factors can also modulate the induction, migration, and immunosuppressive function of FOXP3+ Treg cells. In the context of cancer angiogenesis, VEGF could promote FOXP3+ regulatory T cell migration and its immunosuppressive function. In human cancers, antiangiogenic therapy blocking VEGFR is associated with a reduction of tumour-infiltrating FOXP3+ regulatory T cells. The detailed mechanisms linking VEGFR blockade to this reduction are still unclear. In colorectal cancer tumour microenvironments, tissue-resident FOXP3+ Treg subsets include FOXP3(lo) non-suppressive cells that lack CD45RA, show FOXP3 instability, and secrete inflammatory cytokines such as IFN-γ. Fusobacterium nucleatum and other intestinal bacteria induce tumour production of IL-12, TGF-β, and TNF-α and thereby facilitate expansion of pro-inflammatory FOXP3(lo) non-Treg cells. Expansion of these FOXP3(lo) non-Treg cells enhances anti-tumour immunity and inhibits tumour formation. Colorectal cancer patients with abundant FOXP3(lo) infiltration are predicted to have better survival.
Reference: Qiu, Y., Ke, S., Chen, J., Qin, Z., Zhang, W., Yuan, Y., Meng, D., Zhao, G., Wu, K., Li, B., & Li, D. (2022). FOXP3+ regulatory T cells and the immune escape in solid tumours. Front Immunol, 13, 982986. https://doi.org/10.3389/fimmu.2022.982986

Summary: Foxp3 expression is increased during thymic Treg development and is induced in peripherally generated pTregs following repeated antigen exposure and supportive cytokines. In the thymus, cytokine-dependent upregulation of FOXP3 in CD25+ Treg progenitors constitutes the second step of a two-step developmental process. This process produces mature Tregs that migrate from the thymus and promote tolerance. In the periphery, repeated antigen exposure with sub‑optimal co‑stimulation plus cytokines such as IL‑2 and TGF‑β drives conversion of Foxp3- cells into Foxp3+ pTregs. Intracellularly, FOXP3 is a transcription factor important for the immune regulatory functions of Tregs. The FOXP3 locus has a CpG-rich first intron that is almost uniformly demethylated in tTregs (the Treg-specific demethylated region). FOXP3 engages other transcription factors to drive CD25 and CTLA-4 expression. Demethylation of FOXP3-dependent and some FOXP3-independent genes is required for full Treg development. FOXP3 is the most important transcriptional regulator but it is not sufficient alone to establish the complete suppressive phenotype. In the presence of TGF-β, IL-6 combined with IL-1 induced reprogramming of FOXP3+ Tregs into Th17 effector cells. Under arthritic conditions, FOXP3+ Tregs transitioned to a Th17 phenotype and this process was mediated through IL-6 production by synovial fibroblasts. IFN-γ independently affects Treg stability. IFN-γ promotes conversion into Th1-like cells and increases inflammation. Conversely, ex vivo expansion with IL-6 and TNF-α maintained high FOXP3 expression and low secretion of IL-17, IL-4, and IFN-γ. Alterations in the FOXP3 gene have been linked to autoimmune disease in IPEX patients and scurfy mice.
Reference: Sanders, J. M., Jeyamogan, S., Mathew, J. M., & Leventhal, J. R. (2022). Foxp3+ regulatory T cell therapy for tolerance in autoimmunity and solid organ transplantation. Front Immunol, 13, 1055466. https://doi.org/10.3389/fimmu.2022.1055466

Summary: FOXP3+ regulatory T cells produce FOXP3 and exert immunosuppressive effects that regulate immunity and tumor biology. The cells mature in the thymus as natural regulatory T cells and TGF‑β can induce other T cells to become induced regulatory T cells. Post‑translational modifications are essential for mature FOXP3 function and numerous non‑coding RNAs have been implicated in activating FOXP3 signaling. In gastrointestinal malignancies FOXP3 expression, altered glucose metabolism, and a hypoxic tumor microenvironment influence FOXP3+ Treg function and these mechanisms have been suggested as potential therapeutic targets. In non‑small cell lung cancer FOXP3 marks increased tumor‑infiltrating Tregs compared with normal tissue. Overexpression of FOXP3 in Treg cells in vitro enhances immune‑cell activity and invasiveness, and an increase in FOXP3 levels in the tumor microenvironment may promote tumor cell growth. Advanced NSCLC patients have increased circulating CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ Tregs and their frequency correlates negatively with IL‑17 and positively with IL‑10. In cervical cancer FOXP3 coexpression with VISTA associates with clinical stage and double positivity predicts the worst prognosis. In breast cancer FOXP3 and HAT1 epigenetically enable CCR4 activation in CCR4+ Tregs and FOXP3 overexpression increases CCR4+ Treg infiltration. CCR4+ Treg infiltration is linked to reduced anti‑tumor immunity and tumor progression.
Reference: Wang, W., Ding, M., Wang, Q., Song, Y., Huo, K., Chen, X., Xiang, Z., & Liu, L. (2024). Advances in Foxp3+ regulatory T cells (Foxp3+ Treg) and key factors in digestive malignancies. Front Immunol, 15, 1404974. https://doi.org/10.3389/fimmu.2024.1404974

Summary:
Systematic elaboration present.

Foxp3 is the master transcription factor that regulates regulatory T cell development and is indispensable for maintaining immunity and tolerance. Foxp3 forms multiprotein complexes that activate or repress gene transcription responsible for regulatory T cell differentiation and function. Developing thymic T cells acquire Foxp3 expression, and the level of self-reactivity is thought to support differentiation into thymus-derived tTreg cells. Activated conventional CD4 T cells can acquire Foxp3 under adequate conditions and become peripherally induced pTreg cells. Deletion of Foxp3 CNS-1 impairs TGFβ-dependent Foxp3 induction for iTreg and leads to Th2 mucosal inflammation while not affecting tTreg. Foxp3 deficiency or mutation causes systemic lethal autoimmune inflammation as in scurfy mice and human IPEX syndrome. Some IPEX patients have preserved regulatory T cell generation but exhibit functional regulatory T cell defects. In systemic sclerosis patients, clinicians observed an increased frequency of circulating CD25+FoxP3+CD127− regulatory T cells, especially in early disease. In systemic sclerosis patients, elevated methylation of the FoxP3 promoter inversely correlated with FoxP3 mRNA expression and was accompanied by a reduced proportion of CD4+CD25+FoxP3+ regulatory T cells.
Reference: Lee, J., Kim, D., & Min, B. (2022). Tissue Resident Foxp3+ Regulatory T Cells: Sentinels and Saboteurs in Health and Disease. Front Immunol, 13, 865593. https://doi.org/10.3389/fimmu.2022.865593
